one_line_takeaway: The first empirical (interview-based) study of shadow AI in critical infrastructure finds it is widespread, human-in-the-loop, non-malicious, and largely undetected by existing controls, and frames its cumulative risk as 'assurance erosion' via boundary bypass, unassessed capability expansion, and loss of observability -- rather than as a technical vulnerability or attack.
key_finding: Shadow AI was reported as widespread and organisation-wide across 26 of 27 organisations (one reported no shadow AI usage, attributed to early comprehensive blocking of non-approved tools). Estimates of prevalence among staff ranged as high as 'close to 80 or 90%' in one organisation. Usage was consistently described as interactive, human-in-the-loop, and chat-based, with no evidence of shadow agentic AI or autonomous deployment. Reported use cases centred on document drafting/summarisation, research/information-seeking, coding assistance, and (in a small number of organisations) bespoke local RAG prototypes built without organisational awareness. Commonly named tools were ChatGPT, Claude (for coding), Gemini, Canva, and NotebookLM, alongside sanctioned alternatives (most commonly Microsoft Copilot) that reduced but did not eliminate shadow use. Reported incidents (e.g., bulk document uploads to ChatGPT, sharing of coding artefacts containing personal information) were characterised by participants as limited in severity and non-malicious rather than catastrophic; one organisation reported over 1,300 DLP-triggered events within a single month. DeepSeek was the only AI system explicitly blocked by all organisations, following government advice. The authors conclude shadow AI risk is diffuse, opaque to existing DLP/monitoring, self-reinforcing (repeated low-severity boundary crossings normalise unsafe practice), and incremental/cumulative rather than manifesting as discrete security failures -- motivating their 'assurance erosion' framing over conventional incident-based risk models.
core_contribution: The paper presents what it claims is the first empirical study of 'shadow AI' (informal, unsanctioned use of frontier/generative AI outside organisational controls) specifically in critical infrastructure (CI) organisations. Based on semi-structured interviews with 32 senior executives/functional leaders across 27 Australian CI organisations (Communications, Energy, Water and Sewerage sectors), the authors (i) empirically characterise how shadow AI manifests in CI workplaces, (ii) analyse how it interacts with existing technical, organisational, and governance controls, and (iii) derive an empirically grounded threat model in which shadow AI degrades CI security via three mechanisms: boundary bypass, unassessed capability expansion, and loss of observability via governance circumvention. The paper introduces 'assurance erosion' as a unifying concept describing how these mechanisms cumulatively undermine confidentiality, integrity, accountability, auditability, and observability in regulated CI environments without necessarily causing discrete security incidents.
threat_model: The paper's own threat model (Section V) is explicitly scoped to informal, interactive, human-in-the-loop use of generative AI, explicitly excluding autonomous/agentic AI in production or control workflows (participants reported no evidence of shadow agentic AI). The adversary/actor model is an 'honest-but-curious' or productivity-optimising internal actor -- general staff, privileged users extending tool functionality without formal review, and third-party vendors whose models evolve outside organisational control -- rather than a malicious external or insider adversary. Actors can: (i) submit organisational data to external/embedded AI systems, (ii) use AI to transform/summarise/infer over that data, (iii) introduce or enable new AI capabilities without reassessment, and (iv) integrate AI-generated outputs into workflows without provenance tracking. Three exposure pathways are defined: (1) External Public AI Use / Boundary Bypass -- use of consumer-facing AI services outside approved controls, causing unmonitored data processing and loss of egress visibility; (2) Unassessed AI Capability / Capability Expansion and Use-case Drift -- new AI functionality in sanctioned platforms (often vendor-activated) or repurposing of approved tools beyond assessed scope; (3) Privileged/Local Extension / Governance Circumvention -- technically capable users enabling AI features, local models, or bespoke RAG prototypes outside formal governance and audit trails. Assets at risk extend beyond confidentiality of personal/customer data to decision and knowledge integrity, assurance/auditability, and trust-boundary assumptions. The paper explicitly frames this as a shift from adversarial compromise to systemic 'assurance erosion,' arguing traditional block-or-permit security models are insufficient.
limitations: The authors explicitly state two inherent limitations (Section III-D): (1) the study covers only three CI sectors (Communications, Energy, Water and Sewerage) within Australia's SOCI regulatory regime, and findings may not generalise to other CI sectors such as Healthcare/Medical or Financial Services, or to other regulatory jurisdictions; (2) findings rest on self-reported accounts from senior leadership/governance/strategic roles, which provide a strong enterprise-level view but may under-represent highly localised frontline practices, and reflect partial organisational visibility and institutional framing of risk. The study is explicitly designed for analytic generalisation rather than statistical representativeness (small, purposively sampled N=27 organisations/32 participants). No major security incidents were observed during the study period, so the threat model's claims about escalating risk are extrapolative/interpretive rather than incident-validated. The paper itself does not claim technical/quantitative rigor of a detection or measurement study; it is explicitly a qualitative, interview-based socio-technical analysis.

datasets_used: Semi-structured interview transcripts from 32 participants across 27 Australian critical infrastructure organisations (Communications, Energy, Water and Sewerage sectors), collected December 2025-April 2026; no public dataset or benchmark is used or released
quantitative_results: 26 of 27 organisations reported observed shadow AI usage; 1 reported none, attributed to early comprehensive blocking of non-approved AI tools
One participant estimated shadow AI usage at 'close to 80 or 90% of people' within their organisation
One organisation reported over 1,300 DLP-triggered events related to shadow AI activity within a single month

Participant demographics: 6 Executive Leadership, 12 Senior Management, 7 Digital/Data & AI Leadership, 2 Governance & Policy, 2 Architecture/IT Ops, 2 AI Enablement, 1 Security & Risk; organisations spanned Water & Sewerage (10), Energy (10), Communications (7); org types: Public Sector (13), Private Sector (10), Public-Private (2), Not-for-Profit (2); organisation sizes from <=100 employees (3) to >=5000 employees (2)
baselines_compared: No technical baselines or comparison systems -- this is a qualitative empirical study, not a benchmarked detection/mitigation system
